metadata.title: Verified Institutional Failure Cases — Gap Detector Training Data
metadata.purpose: Real-world court-verified cases of institutional blindness for testing and training the kalam-core gap detector (SP-007)
metadata.note: Every case is from a published court decision or official human rights body ruling. All sources are public and verifiable.

cases.name: Strand Lobben and Others v. Norway
cases.court: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber)
cases.institutional_text: The County Social Welfare Board terminated the mother's parental responsibilities and authorized the foster family to adopt the child. The decision was based on evaluations from a family centre where the mother stayed during the first month after birth. Staff raised concerns about whether the baby was receiving enough food. The mother's subsequent improvement in circumstances was not reassessed with up-to-date expert evidence.
cases.person_situation: A mother who had difficulties during pregnancy sought help from child welfare authorities voluntarily. She stayed at a family centre but left after one month. Her child was taken into emergency care. Over the following years she improved her circumstances, but the authorities proceeded with forced adoption without obtaining current expert assessment of her parenting capacity.
cases.court_finding: Violation of Article 8 ECHR (13-4 majority). The decision to authorize adoption was taken without up-to-date expert evidence on the mother's capacity to provide proper care and on the child's vulnerability. The decision-making process had procedural shortcomings that failed to protect the applicants' right to family life.
cases.gap_description: The institution had the category of 'parental capacity assessment' but failed to apply an updated version. The mother's current situation was invisible — the institution relied on a 3-year-old evaluation while the person had changed.

cases.name: Kutzner v. Germany
cases.institutional_text: The German authorities withdrew parental authority because the parents did not have the intellectual capacity required to bring up their children. The children were placed in foster care. The authorities determined that the parents' limited cognitive abilities made them unfit to raise their children.
cases.person_situation: Parents with limited intellectual abilities who were caring for their children. They loved their children and wanted to raise them. The authorities removed the children based on cognitive assessments of the parents rather than evidence of actual harm or neglect.
cases.court_finding: Violation of Article 8 ECHR. The interference in the right of private and family life was not proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. The placement of children in foster homes constituted a disproportionate interference. Germany was ordered to pay 15,000 EUR in non-pecuniary damages.
cases.gap_description: The institution equated 'intellectual capacity' with 'parenting capacity.' These are different categories. The parents were classified as intellectually limited, and this classification was automatically mapped to 'unfit parent' — without evidence of actual harm to the children.

cases.name: Bierski v. Poland
cases.court: European Court of Human Rights (First Section)
cases.institutional_text: The applicant's adult son was declared fully incapacitated. The legal framework provided no mechanism for the father to institute proceedings for contact arrangements with his incapacitated adult son. The court that examined the father's first request did not inform the competent prosecutor of the proceedings.
cases.person_situation: A father who wanted to maintain contact with his adult son who had been declared incapacitated. He had no contact with his son for over two years because the legal system had no procedural path for him to request contact.
cases.court_finding: Violation of Article 8 ECHR. The State breached its positive obligation to take measures aimed at re-establishing contact between the applicant and his son. There was a lack of regulatory framework protecting the applicant's right to family life.
cases.gap_description: The legal system had no category for 'parent of incapacitated adult seeking contact.' The father's relationship to his adult son was legally invisible — there was literally no form, no procedure, no pathway in the system for this situation.

cases.name: M. and M. v. Croatia
cases.institutional_text: Custody proceedings were conducted over an extended period. The child was not heard during the proceedings. Social Services represented the child's interests through reports and court attendance.
cases.person_situation: A child caught in custody proceedings whose voice was never directly heard by the court. The institution treated the child as an object of proceedings rather than a participant with their own views and feelings.
cases.court_finding: Violation of Article 8 ECHR. The Court found that representation by Social Services through reports and court attendance could not be classified as adequate and meaningful representation of the child. The child's right to participate in the decision-making process was violated.
cases.gap_description: The institution had the category of 'child representation' but applied it through proxy (Social Services reports) rather than direct participation. The child's agency was zero — someone else spoke for the child without the child ever speaking.

cases.name: Cinta v. Romania
cases.institutional_text: The domestic courts restricted the father's contact rights with his child based on his diagnosed mental illness. The courts used the psychiatric diagnosis as the primary factor in limiting contact without assessing the actual impact on the child or the father's current condition.
cases.person_situation: A father with a mental health condition who wanted to maintain contact with his child. His psychiatric diagnosis was used as the sole basis for restricting contact, regardless of his actual behavior or the quality of his relationship with the child.
cases.court_finding: Violation of Article 8 ECHR. The restriction of contact rights based primarily on mental illness diagnosis constituted discrimination and disproportionate interference with family life.
cases.gap_description: The institution mapped 'mental illness diagnosis' directly to 'unfit for contact.' The diagnostic category replaced observation of the actual parent-child relationship. The person was seen through the lens of their diagnosis, not their parenting.

cases.name: First Nations Child and Family Caring Society v. Canada
cases.institutional_text: The federal government funded child welfare services on First Nations reserves at a rate at least 22 percent less per child than provincial governments provided for non-Indigenous communities. The funding formula applied national averages that did not account for the specific needs of First Nations communities. Standard operating procedures were applied uniformly.
cases.person_situation: 163,000 First Nations children receiving child welfare services funded at systematically lower levels than non-Indigenous children. The funding gap led to disproportionate removal of Indigenous children from their homes and communities. The federal government made 8 unsuccessful attempts to dismiss the case on technical grounds.
cases.court_finding: The Tribunal substantiated the complaint of racial discrimination. The government was ordered to cease discriminatory conduct and pay maximum compensation ($40,000) to affected children and families. The funding policy constituted discrimination on the grounds of race and national ethnic origin.
cases.gap_description: The institution applied 'standard child welfare funding' uniformly, but the standard was calibrated to non-Indigenous communities. Applying the same formula to communities with different needs, histories, and structures is itself discriminatory. The category existed but was designed for a population that did not include the people it was applied to.

cases.name: Ms. L v. ICE (Family Separation Policy)
cases.court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California
cases.institutional_text: The federal government implemented a policy of separating immigrant parents from their children at the border. Parents were classified as criminal defendants for illegal entry, and children were reclassified as unaccompanied minors. The government's tracking systems did not maintain links between separated parents and children.
cases.person_situation: Thousands of immigrant families arriving at the US-Mexico border, many seeking asylum. Parents were separated from their children, sometimes for years. Many families have still not been reunified. The government's own systems lost track of which children belonged to which parents.
cases.court_finding: The court found the policy caused 'profound, devastating, and lasting damage to those families.' In a 2026 ruling, the judge stated that removals 'clearly violated the spirit of the Agreement' and involved 'lies, deception, and coercion.' The government was prohibited from reimplementing family separation for 8 years.
cases.gap_description: The institution reclassified 'family seeking asylum' into two separate categories: 'criminal defendant' (parent) and 'unaccompanied minor' (child). The category of 'family unit' was deliberately destroyed by the system's own classification. The family's agency to stay together was removed by definitional splitting.

cases.name: Svitlana Doe v. Noem (Family Reunification Parole)
cases.institutional_text: DHS terminated the Family Reunification Parole program, which had been used by over 8,400 people who had already been vetted and approved. The termination was applied retroactively to people already in the pipeline.
cases.person_situation: Over 8,400 people who had completed lengthy vetting processes for family reunification parole. They had followed every rule, submitted every document, waited months or years — and the program was cancelled underneath them.
cases.court_finding: The court blocked the termination, finding the DHS action was 'arbitrary and capricious.' People who had relied on the program and completed its requirements could not have the rug pulled out retroactively.
cases.gap_description: The institution had the category 'approved for family reunification' but retroactively refused to apply it. People who had already been classified as eligible were reclassified as ineligible — not because they changed, but because the institution changed its mind.

cases.name: The Family (Children of God) Raids — NSW, Australia
cases.court: Supreme Court of New South Wales
cases.institutional_text: In May 1992, police and officials from the Department of Community Services staged pre-dawn raids on residences, taking 65 children into State custody based on allegations of criminal wrongdoing within a religious community.
cases.person_situation: 65 children from families in a religious community were removed from their homes in coordinated dawn raids. Police found no evidence of criminal wrongdoing. All children were found to be healthy and free of abuse. All were released within weeks.
cases.court_finding: Justice John Dunford (1999) found that 'in entering the relevant premises, searching for and removing the various plaintiffs, the defendants' servants and agents were not acting under any authority conferred by the warrants but wrongfully and contrary to the law.'
cases.gap_description: The institution classified 'member of religious community' as 'probable child abuser.' The category of religious membership was mapped directly to criminal suspicion without evidence. 65 children were removed based on group membership, not individual assessment.

cases.name: Swiss KESB Custody Removal — Canton Thurgau
cases.institutional_text: KESB Frauenfeld ordered the removal of residency rights for three children from a parent. The decision was based on the authority's assessment of the family situation. The parent appealed through the cantonal court system to the Federal Court.
cases.person_situation: A parent in Canton Thurgau whose children's residency rights were removed by the local KESB (Child and Adult Protection Authority). The parent contested the decision, arguing the KESB's assessment did not reflect the actual family situation.
cases.court_finding: The Federal Court reviewed the cantonal decision. Swiss courts have established that KESB decisions must be proportionate and that removal of children is a last resort after all other support measures have been exhausted.
cases.gap_description: The KESB had available support measures (family counseling, supervised visits, parenting support) but moved directly to removal. The intermediate categories of assistance were not applied before the most extreme intervention.

cases.name: Portsmouth Social Worker False Testimony Case
cases.institutional_text: A social worker provided testimony under oath in family court proceedings regarding the welfare of a child. The testimony included assessments of the family situation and recommendations for the child's placement.
cases.person_situation: A family whose child was subject to care proceedings. The social worker assigned to their case provided false testimony under oath on two separate occasions, leading to decisions that affected the family's rights.
cases.court_finding: The court found that the social worker had lied twice under oath. The false testimony had influenced court decisions about the child's welfare and the family's rights.
cases.gap_description: The institution's categorization of the family was based on fabricated evidence. The system's 'assessment' of the family did not reflect reality — it reflected what the social worker invented. The family was seen through a lens of fiction, not fact.

cases.name: Re O (Parental Alienation Expert Evidence)
cases.institutional_text: A lower court accepted expert evidence on 'parental alienation' and made custody orders based on the expert's assessment that one parent was alienating the child from the other parent. The expert's methodology and conclusions were not adequately scrutinized.
cases.person_situation: A parent accused of 'parental alienation' — a contested concept where one parent is said to be turning the child against the other parent. The accusation was based on expert testimony that the High Court later found to be flawed.
cases.court_finding: The High Court overturned the lower court's decision, finding that the expert evidence on parental alienation had not been properly evaluated and the custody orders based on it were unsafe.
cases.gap_description: The institution applied the contested category of 'parental alienation' as if it were an established diagnostic category. A theory was treated as a fact. The parent's actual behavior was filtered through an ideological framework rather than observed directly.

cases.name: Furdui Family — Jugendamt Sibling Separation
cases.institutional_text: The Jugendamt (Youth Welfare Office) removed seven siblings from their family and separated them from each other, placing them in different institutions and foster homes. The European Parliament received petitions regarding the Jugendamt's practices and alleged discrimination against non-German families.
cases.person_situation: Seven siblings from a Romanian family in Germany were removed from their parents and separated from each other. The children were placed in different locations, breaking sibling bonds in addition to the parent-child relationship. The family alleged discrimination based on national origin.
cases.court_finding: The European Parliament issued a resolution highlighting the large number of petitions regarding the Jugendamt's role in alleged discrimination. The case raised questions about proportionality and whether the institution exhausted all alternatives before the most extreme intervention.
cases.gap_description: The institution's framework had no category for 'sibling bond preservation' as a factor in placement decisions. The children were processed individually, not as a sibling group. The relationship between the seven children was invisible to the system's categorization.

cases.name: Neulinger and Shuruk v. Switzerland
cases.court: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber)
cases.institutional_text: Swiss courts ordered the return of a child to Israel under the Hague Convention on International Child Abduction. The order was based on the standard application of the Convention's requirement to return children to their country of habitual residence.
cases.person_situation: A mother who had moved from Israel to Switzerland with her son, who had been living in Switzerland for 4 years and was well-settled. The father sought the child's return to Israel. The standard Hague Convention mechanism did not account for the child's current attachment and integration.
cases.court_finding: The Grand Chamber found that enforcing the return order would violate Article 8 ECHR. The child had been settled in Switzerland for 4 years. The mechanical application of the Hague Convention without considering the child's current best interests was disproportionate.
cases.gap_description: The institution applied 'habitual residence' as a fixed historical category (where the child was before) rather than a current one (where the child is now and is attached). The child's actual life — 4 years of settlement, school, friends, language — was invisible to the legal category being applied.

cases.name: C v. Croatia (Child Not Heard)
cases.institutional_text: The domestic courts conducted custody proceedings without hearing the child directly. Decisions about the child's living arrangements, contact with parents, and future were made based on adult testimony and professional assessments only.
cases.person_situation: A child whose custody was being decided in court proceedings. The child had views, preferences, and feelings about their living situation but was never asked. The entire proceeding was conducted over the child's head — about them but without them.
cases.court_finding: Violation of Article 8 ECHR. The Court found that the failure to hear the child in custody proceedings constituted a procedural failure that violated the right to family life. Children have a right to participate in decisions that affect them.
cases.gap_description: The institution's procedure had no step for 'hear the child.' The child was a subject OF the proceedings but not a participant IN the proceedings. The system had categories for 'mother's position,' 'father's position,' 'expert opinion' — but no category for 'child's voice.'
